title: Economic growth & development
summary:
## The big idea A country's GDP can double while its villages still lack clean water. **Growth** and **development** are related but distinct — and the difference organises everything in this chapter: - **Economic growth** = a rise in **real GDP** — *quantitative*, about output. - **Economic development** = a broad, *qualitative* improvement in **welfare and quality of life**: living standards, health, education, choice, freedom, reduced poverty. Growth is usually necessary for development — but not sufficient: gains can be captured by a narrow elite, bought with environmental destruction, or concentrated in an enclave (an oil platform) touching few lives.
**What each part means:** the solid **PPC1** is today's capacity. Investment, education, technology and better institutions push it out to the dashed **PPC2** — the economy *can* now produce more of everything: **potential growth** (identical to the LRAS shifting right). Development asks the further questions the diagram can't show: *who* gets the extra output, *what* it does to health and schooling, and whether the growth is sustainable.
## Measuring development — beyond GDP - **The Human Development Index (HDI)** blends three dimensions into a 0–1 score: **income** (GNI per capita at PPP), **health** (life expectancy at birth) and **education** (mean and expected years of schooling). It reshuffles the world's rankings — resource-rich states slide down; well-schooled, healthy middle-income countries climb. Limits: it still ignores **inequality, environment, freedom and security** (a country can score well while jailing dissidents). - Complements: the **Multidimensional Poverty Index**, access to clean water and sanitation, the Gini coefficient, internet/mobile penetration, and wellbeing surveys. ## What developing economies tend to have in common With huge variation: low GDP per head, fast **population growth**, dependence on **primary products**, large **informal and agricultural sectors**, low savings and investment, thin infrastructure, weak human capital, and fragile institutions. Treat these as *tendencies*, not a checklist — Bangladesh, Botswana and Bolivia are all 'developing' and utterly different. ## Barriers to growth and development - **The savings gap (Harrod–Domar logic):** growth needs investment; investment needs savings; poor countries can't save → **the vicious cycle of poverty** (low income → low saving → low investment → low income). Foreign capital, aid or FDI can plug the gap — each with strings attached. - **Primary-product dependency:** commodity prices are volatile (boom–bust budgets) and, per the **Prebisch–Singer hypothesis**, tend to decline long-run against manufactured goods — the **terms of trade** slowly turn against raw-material exporters. Resource wealth can even *hurt* via **Dutch disease**: a commodity boom inflates the exchange rate and strangles other exports. - **Human capital deficits** — sick, under-schooled workforces are unproductive (and the loss loops back into poverty). - **Institutions** — corruption, weak property rights, unstable politics and conflict deter every investor, domestic and foreign. - **Debt burdens** — servicing old loans eats the budget that should build schools. - **Demographics** — population outrunning capital ('capital shallowing'). ## Strategies — and their catch - **Invest in physical & human capital**; court **FDI** (brings capital, technology, jobs — but profits repatriate, and enclaves may not spread). - **Trade strategy:** **export-led growth** (the East Asian route — South Korea from war ruin to rich in two generations) vs **import substitution** (protect home industries — mostly disappointing: cosseted firms stayed inefficient). **Diversification** away from primary products is the shared goal. - **Aid and debt relief** — can fund the savings gap; risks dependency, tied conditions and corruption leakage. **Microfinance** attacks the credit barrier bottom-up (Bangladesh's Grameen model) — helpful, not transformative. - **The Lewis model:** development as the transfer of **surplus labour** from low-productivity agriculture into industry — a decent sketch of China's urbanisation, less apt where manufacturing never ignites. - **Institutional reform** — property rights, courts, anti-corruption: unglamorous, slow, and by Botswana's example, decisive. **Evaluation reflexes:** no strategy works everywhere (compare **South Korea** and **Nigeria**); sequencing and state capacity matter; and **appropriate technology** beats prestige projects. ## Sustainability **Sustainable development** meets today's needs **without compromising future generations** — growth that fells its forests, empties its aquifers and cooks its climate is borrowing welfare from its grandchildren, and GDP will never show the debt. This links straight to externalities (Unit 6) and the environmental caveats on growth (Unit 5/10). ## Why it matters Development questions reward *cases*: **South Korea** (export-led success), **Nigeria** (oil dependency and Dutch disease), **Bangladesh** (garment-led growth + microfinance), **Botswana** (diamonds *plus* institutions — resource wealth done right). Contrasting two cases in an essay is the fastest route to top-band application.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Growth** = more real GDP (quantitative); **development** = better lives (qualitative). Growth ≠ development. - **HDI** = income (GNI/head PPP) + health (life expectancy) + education (schooling years); 0–1; ignores inequality, environment, freedom. - Barriers: **savings gap** / vicious cycle, **primary-product dependency** (Prebisch–Singer, Dutch disease), human capital, institutions/corruption, debt, demographics. - Strategies: investment & FDI, **export-led growth** vs import substitution, diversification, aid/debt relief/microfinance, **Lewis model**, institutional reform. - Every strategy has a catch — dependency, repatriated profits, inappropriate technology, sustainability. - Cases to deploy: **South Korea, Nigeria, Bangladesh, Botswana**.

keyTerms.definition: A quantitative increase in real GDP (output).
A qualitative improvement in welfare, living standards and quality of life.
A 0–1 composite of income, health and education used to measure development.
Low incomes leading to low savings and investment, limiting growth.
Reliance on exporting raw commodities with volatile, often declining, prices.
The long-run tendency for the terms of trade of primary exporters to decline.
Development that meets present needs without compromising future generations.
Investment by multinationals in productive assets in another country.

diagrams: Use the **PPC / AD-LRAS** to show growth (outward shift), and note development goes beyond these output measures.
Optional: a **vicious cycle of poverty** flow diagram (low income → low saving → low investment → low growth).
tips: State the **growth vs development** distinction up front — quantitative output vs qualitative welfare.
Explain **HDI's three components** and its limitations (ignores inequality, environment, freedom).
Use named **barriers** (savings gap, primary-product dependency, human capital, institutions) and matching **strategies**.
Always **evaluate strategies** (aid dependency, sustainability, appropriate technology) rather than listing them.
Apply real developing economies — **contrast South Korea (success), Nigeria (primary-product dependency) and Bangladesh (garment-led growth)** — for strong application/evaluation marks.

channels.name: EconplusDal
channels.why: Clear on HDI, barriers and development strategies.

keyConcepts.title: Economic growth vs development and HDI

revisionNotes:
# Growth & development — quick revision

**The distinction**
- Growth = ↑ real GDP (quantitative). Development = ↑ welfare/quality of life (qualitative: health, education, freedom, poverty).
- Growth is necessary-ish but **not sufficient** (inequality, enclaves, environment).

**Measurement**
- **HDI (0–1)**: GNI/head (PPP) + life expectancy + schooling (mean & expected years).
- Limits: no inequality, environment, freedom. Complements: MPI, water/sanitation, Gini.

**Barriers (name + mechanism)**
- **Savings gap** → vicious cycle of poverty (Harrod–Domar).
- **Primary-product dependency**: volatility + **Prebisch–Singer** declining terms of trade + **Dutch disease**.
- Weak human capital; weak **institutions**/corruption; debt service; population growth.

**Strategies (each with its catch)**
- Physical/human capital investment; **FDI** (profits repatriate; enclaves).
- **Export-led growth** (South Korea) vs import substitution (mostly failed); diversification.
- Aid & debt relief (dependency, conditions); microfinance (Grameen — modest).
- **Lewis model**: farm → factory labour transfer (China-ish).
- Institutions: property rights, courts, anti-corruption (Botswana).

**Sustainability** — meet present needs without robbing future generations; GDP hides resource depletion.

**Cases** — South Korea (success), Nigeria (oil curse), Bangladesh (garments), Botswana (institutions).
questions:
# Practice questions — Economic growth & development **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** Which of the following best illustrates economic development rather than merely economic growth? - A — real GDP rises 5% due to higher oil exports - B — infant mortality falls and average years of schooling rise - C — nominal GDP doubles during high inflation - D — the population increases
▸ Show answer

B. Development is about welfare and capabilities — health and education — not just output. A is growth (possibly enclave); C may involve no real change at all.

**2.** The three components of the Human Development Index are: - A — income, inequality and unemployment - B — GNI per capita, life expectancy and years of schooling - C — GDP, inflation and the trade balance - D — savings, investment and exports
▸ Show answer

B. Income (GNI/head at PPP), health (life expectancy) and education (mean + expected schooling years), combined into a 0–1 index.

**3.** The Prebisch–Singer hypothesis predicts that countries dependent on primary-product exports will experience: - A — improving terms of trade over time - B — deteriorating terms of trade over time - C — constant terms of trade - D — rising export prices relative to import prices
▸ Show answer

B. Demand for primary products grows slowly (low YED) while manufactured import prices trend up — so the price ratio moves against commodity exporters long-run.

**4.** The 'vicious cycle of poverty' argument holds that poor countries stay poor because: - A — low incomes prevent the saving needed to finance investment - B — they lack natural resources - C — their exchange rates are fixed - D — foreign aid causes dependency
▸ Show answer

A. Low income → low saving → low investment → low productivity → low income: the savings-gap loop. Breaking it is the case for FDI, aid or forced saving.

## Paper 4 style — data response > Country X earns 85% of export revenue from crude oil. During the last oil boom its currency appreciated 30%, and its once-promising textile industry shrank by half. Oil revenues flow through a state company described by auditors as 'opaque'; school completion rates and life expectancy have barely moved in twenty years despite GDP per head doubling. Its HDI rank is 40 places below its income rank. The government now proposes: a sovereign wealth fund, spending rules, and subsidies to agro-processing industries. **(a)** Define 'economic development'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why Country X's HDI rank sits so far below its income rank. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how the oil boom damaged the textile industry. **[6]** **(d)** Assess the government's three proposals for turning oil wealth into development. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A qualitative, broad improvement in welfare and quality of life — health, education, living standards, choices — beyond mere output growth. [1+1]

(b) HDI adds health and education to income: X's income is high (oil) but life expectancy and schooling stagnate — the non-income components drag its rank down; income poorly distributed/converted into services (opaque state company). [4]

(c) **Dutch disease**: oil exports → currency demand → 30% appreciation → textiles priced out of export markets (SPICED) → industry halves; resources and investment also pulled into the booming sector. Chain fully developed. [6]

(d) Wealth fund: saves windfalls abroad → sterilises exchange-rate pressure + smooths volatility (Norway model) — but needs the very transparency X lacks. Spending rules: counter boom–bust budgeting; enforcement is the question. Agro-processing subsidies: diversification logic, but picking winners + subsidy capture risks; institutions first (audit the state company). Judgement: right package **if** governance reform comes with it — otherwise the curse continues. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why economic growth may fail to bring economic development. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the usefulness of the HDI compared with GDP per capita as a measure of development. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Distribution (elite capture), enclave sectors, environmental costs, output composition (arms vs schools), population growth diluting per-head gains — any three developed.

(b) HDI advantages: multidimensional, exposes income–welfare gaps, comparable. Limits: omits inequality, environment, freedom; component weighting arbitrary; data quality. GDP/head: simple, timely, but one-dimensional. Judgement: HDI better for development, best used alongside distribution and sustainability indicators.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain **two** barriers that make it difficult for the poorest countries to grow. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether export-led growth is the most effective development strategy available to a developing economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) E.g. savings gap (vicious cycle) and primary-product dependency (volatility + Prebisch–Singer) — or human capital/institutions; mechanisms spelled out.

(b) For: East Asian record (South Korea, later Bangladesh's garments) — scale economies, foreign exchange, competition discipline, jobs. Against: needs infrastructure and stable policy; world demand and protectionism outside its control; race-to-the-bottom wage risks; import substitution mostly failed but selective infant-industry support played a role in Korea itself. Judgement: strongest single strategy, but conditional on institutions and diversification.
